title: Topic 371: Why Some Games Fail in Certain Markets: Cultural Mismatch
content:
# Topic 371: Why Some Games Fail in Certain Markets: Cultural Mismatch

## ABSTRACT

This paper explores the fascinating and often hilarious phenomenon of video games flopping in foreign markets due to cultural mismatch. We delve into the graveyard of digital dreams, where well-intentioned games go to die, not with a bang, but with a confused whimper from a culturally bewildered audience. Through a rigorous analysis of localization blunders, marketing misfires, and gameplay mechanics that just don't translate, we reveal the secret sauce of cross-cultural success in the gaming industry. Or, at the very least, we'll have a good laugh at the expense of those who got it spectacularly wrong. This paper is a must-read for anyone who has ever wondered why their favorite game was a colossal failure in Japan, or why a seemingly brilliant marketing campaign was met with stony silence in Germany.

## INTRODUCTION

In the global village of the 21st century, where a tweet can circumnavigate the globe faster than you can say "localization," one would assume that cultural barriers are a thing of the past. And yet, the video game industry, a multi-billion dollar behemoth that prides itself on connecting people from all corners of the world, is littered with the digital corpses of games that failed to cross the cultural divide. These are not games that were bad, per se. Many were critical darlings in their home countries, lauded for their innovative gameplay, compelling narratives, and stunning visuals. But when they ventured abroad, they were met with the cultural equivalent of a blank stare. It’s a phenomenon as old as trade itself, but in the fast-paced, high-stakes world of video games, the consequences of a cultural misstep can be catastrophic, leading to financial ruin and, in some cases, the closure of entire studios. This paper will explore the myriad reasons why some games fail to resonate with audiences in different cultural contexts. We will examine the obvious culprits, such as botched translations and culturally insensitive content, but we will also delve into the more subtle and insidious factors at play, such as differing attitudes towards violence, humor, and even the very concept of fun. We will also explore the psychological underpinnings of these cultural differences, drawing on theories from cross-cultural psychology and consumer behavior to shed light on why what is considered a masterpiece in one country can be a laughingstock in another. So, grab your popcorn (or your culturally appropriate snack of choice) as we embark on a journey into the heart of cultural mismatch in the video game industry. It’s a wild ride, full of unexpected twists, turns, and, of course, a healthy dose of schadenfreude.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the issue of cultural mismatch in video games. While the field is still relatively young, a growing body of research has begun to explore the complex interplay between culture and gaming. One of the foundational concepts in this area is the idea of "localization," which refers to the process of adapting a game for a specific cultural market. As Carlson and Corliss (2011) argue, localization is not simply a matter of translation, but a complex process of cultural filtering and adaptation. They contend that localizers act as "intermediaries" who shape the images and narratives that are presented to global consumers, and that this process is often guided by "mythologies of cultural difference" that may or may not reflect the reality of the target culture. [1] This is a polite way of saying that sometimes, the people in charge of making a game palatable to a foreign audience have about as much cultural sensitivity as a bull in a china shop. Other scholars have focused on the more practical aspects of localization, such as the challenges of translating humor, slang, and cultural references. As one article on gaming translation fails points out, context is king. In the early days of localization, it was common for developers to simply hand over a spreadsheet of text to a translator with no context whatsoever, leading to such immortal lines as "All your base are belong to us" from the game *Zero Wing*. [2] While this particular example has achieved a sort of cult status, it is a stark reminder of the importance of providing translators with the tools and information they need to do their job effectively. More recent research has begun to explore the deeper psychological and sociological factors that contribute to cultural mismatch in gaming. For example, some studies have examined how cultural values, such as individualism and collectivism, can influence player behavior and preferences. Others have looked at how different cultures perceive and react to violence, sexuality, and other sensitive topics in games. The general consensus is that there is no one-size-fits-all approach to game design and marketing. What works in one culture may not work in another, and developers who fail to take this into account do so at their own peril.

## ANALYSIS

So, why do some games get lost in translation? The reasons are as varied and complex as the cultures themselves, but they can be broadly categorized into three main areas: language, culture, and gameplay. Language is the most obvious barrier, and the one that is most often blamed for localization failures. But as we have already seen, the problem is not just about getting the words right. It’s about getting the tone, the style, and the cultural context right. A literal translation of a joke is rarely funny, and a direct translation of a cultural reference can be utterly meaningless to someone from a different background. But the challenges of localization go far beyond language. Cultural values and norms also play a huge role in determining how a game is received. For example, a game that is considered to be a harmless bit of fun in one culture might be seen as deeply offensive in another. This is particularly true when it comes to issues of religion, politics, and history. A game that features a swastika, for example, would be an instant no-go in Germany, where the symbol is banned. Similarly, a game that depicts a historical event in a way that is seen as inaccurate or disrespectful is likely to be met with a backlash from the culture in question. But even seemingly innocuous cultural differences can have a big impact on a game’s success. For example, the way that characters are designed can be a major point of contention. The infamous case of the *Ratchet and Clank* box art being changed for the Japanese market is a perfect example of this. The original art was deemed to be too aggressive for Japanese tastes, so the characters were redrawn in a more "cutesy" anime style. [1] Finally, we have gameplay. This is perhaps the most subtle and difficult aspect of cultural mismatch to pin down, but it is also one of the most important. Different cultures have different ideas about what constitutes a fun and engaging gameplay experience. For example, some cultures prefer games that are fast-paced and action-packed, while others prefer games that are more slow-paced and strategic. Some cultures enjoy games that are incredibly difficult and require a high level of skill, while others prefer games that are more forgiving and accessible. These differences in gameplay preferences can be traced back to a variety of factors, including cultural values, educational systems, and even the amount of leisure time that people have. Whatever the reason, the fact remains that a gameplay mechanic that is beloved in one culture can be a complete turn-off in another.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The video game industry is littered with the corpses of games that have fallen victim to cultural mismatch. One of the most famous examples is the case of *E.T. the Extra-Terrestrial*, a game that was so bad it is often blamed for the video game crash of 1983. [3] While the game’s failure was due to a variety of factors, including a rushed development cycle and poor gameplay, it is also a classic example of a game that failed to understand its target audience. The game was designed to appeal to a broad, family-friendly audience, but its obtuse and frustrating gameplay was a complete turn-off for most players. Another classic example of a cultural mismatch is the case of *Afro Samurai 2: Revenge of Kuma*. This game, which was based on a popular manga and anime series, was a critical and commercial disaster. [3] The game was panned for its poor controls, repetitive gameplay, and technical issues. But the game’s failure was also due to a fundamental misunderstanding of the source material. The game’s developers failed to capture the style, the tone, and the spirit of the original series, and as a result, they alienated the very fans they were trying to attract. More recently, we have the case of *Anthem*, a game that was hyped as the next big thing in the world of online gaming. But when the game was released, it was met with a lukewarm reception from critics and players alike. [3] The game was criticized for its lack of content, its repetitive missions, and its generic story. But the game’s failure was also due to a cultural mismatch within the gaming community itself. The game was designed to appeal to a broad, mainstream audience, but it failed to understand the needs and desires of the hardcore gamers who make up the core of the online gaming community. These are just a few examples of the many games that have failed due to cultural mismatch. But they all serve as a cautionary tale for developers who are thinking of taking their games to a global audience. The lesson is clear: do your homework, understand your audience, and for the love of all that is holy, don’t assume that what works in your own backyard will work everywhere else.

## DISCUSSION

The phenomenon of cultural mismatch in video games has a number of important implications for our understanding of human behavior and consumer habits. First and foremost, it is a powerful reminder that we are not all the same. Despite the homogenizing forces of globalization, cultural differences are still very much alive and well. And these differences have a profound impact on the way that we think, feel, and behave. This is particularly true when it comes to our entertainment choices. What we find to be fun, engaging, and meaningful is deeply rooted in our cultural background. And what is considered to be a masterpiece in one culture can be a complete and utter bore in another. This has important implications for the video game industry, of course. But it also has broader implications for any business that is looking to operate in a global market. The lesson is clear: if you want to succeed in a foreign market, you need to do more than just translate your marketing materials. You need to understand the culture of your target audience, and you need to adapt your products and services accordingly. But the phenomenon of cultural mismatch in video games also has a more personal and philosophical implication. It is a reminder that our own cultural perspective is not the only one. There are other ways of seeing the world, other ways of having fun, and other ways of being human. And by exposing ourselves to these different cultural perspectives, we can broaden our own horizons and enrich our own lives. So, the next time you see a game that has been a colossal failure in a foreign market, don’t just laugh at the developers’ expense. Take a moment to consider the cultural factors that might have contributed to its failure. You might just learn something about the world, and about yourself.

## CONCLUSION

In conclusion, the failure of some games in certain markets due to cultural mismatch is a complex and multifaceted phenomenon. It is a reminder that the world is not a monoculture, and that what is considered to be a masterpiece in one country can be a complete and utter disaster in another. The reasons for these failures are as varied as the cultures themselves, ranging from botched translations and culturally insensitive content to more subtle differences in gameplay preferences and expectations. But whatever the reason, the lesson is clear: if you want to succeed in the global video game market, you need to do your homework. You need to understand the culture of your target audience, and you need to be willing to adapt your game accordingly. And if you’re not willing to do that, then you’re better off staying in your own backyard. Because as we have seen, the graveyard of digital dreams is littered with the corpses of games that failed to cross the cultural divide. And there’s always room for one more.
references:
[1] Carlson, R., & Corliss, J. (2011). Imagined commodities: Video game localization and mythologies of cultural difference. *Games and Culture*, *6*(1), 61-82.

[2] Localize. (n.d.). *The Greatest Gaming Translation Fails*. Retrieved from https://localizejs.com/articles/greatest-gaming-localization-fails

[3] Wikipedia. (n.d.). *List of commercial failures in video games*. Retrieved from https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_commercial_failures_in_video_and_arcade_games
